message:
feat(rebase-recovery): narrowly unblock `git checkout --` / `git
restore` when the user is actively recovering from a failed
`git pull --rebase` (issue #104)

AI agents hit a workflow dead-end whenever `git pull --rebase` blew up
partway — stash-pop conflicts, interrupted rebases, or the classic
"unstaged changes, cannot pull with rebase" loop. The next step is
almost always `git checkout -- .` or `git restore <paths>`, both of
which dcg hard-blocks (`core.git:checkout-discard`,
`core.git:restore-worktree`). Result: agents stop and ask a human to
run the command by hand, or worse, retry `git pull` in circles.

Add `rebase-recovery` mode — a narrow, bounded relaxation of those
two rules that only fires under a genuine recovery signal. Outside
that signal the default block is unchanged.

### Two complementary signals unlock recovery

1. **Active rebase state (automatic, zero-config).** When
   `.git/rebase-merge/` or `.git/rebase-apply/` exists, a rebase is
   in progress and the discard operations are the documented
   recovery path. dcg detects this state and converts the deny into
   an allow with a stderr note, no cookie required.

2. **Explicit permit cookie (opt-in, short-lived).** The agent (or
   user) runs `dcg rebase-recover`, which writes a timestamp to
   `.dcg/rebase-recovery-permit` at the repo root. For the next 120
   seconds (default; capped at 600 via the new subcommand's `--ttl`
   flag), `git checkout --` and `git restore` are allowed *and the
   permit is consumed on first use* — a true single-shot cookie, so
   subsequent unrelated destructive commands stay blocked.

### Files

* `src/rebase_recovery.rs` (new, 433 lines) — the module. Exposes
  `should_allow_recovery(cwd, pack, pattern) ->
  Option<RecoveryReason>` and `consume_permit(cwd)`. Detects the
  repo root by walking up to the nearest `.git/`, recognises both
  `rebase-merge` and `rebase-apply` state dirs, parses permit TTL
  with a hard cap at 600s, and emits an `ActiveRebase |
  ActivePermit(ttl_remaining)` reason so `main.rs` can render a
  human-readable label. Includes exhaustive unit tests for the
  detector logic.

* `src/lib.rs` — `pub mod rebase_recovery;` so both `main.rs` and
  out-of-process call sites (tests, future subcommands) can reach
  the detector.

* `src/cli.rs` — new subcommand
  `#[command(name = "rebase-recover")]` with an optional `--ttl
  <SECONDS>` arg, plus the dispatch arm that calls
  `handle_rebase_recover(ttl, robot_mode)`. The handler is 60+
  lines and is the thing that actually writes the permit file; it
  respects `cli.robot || DCG_ROBOT` for silent success and emits
  a JSON or human-readable confirmation accordingly.

* `src/main.rs` — pre-emit check right before a `DecisionMode::Deny`
  would have been committed to disk. If the matched pattern is on
  the recovery allowlist AND `should_allow_recovery` returns
  `Some(reason)`:
    - Log a history entry with outcome `Allow` and reason-tag
      `"rebase-recovery"` so audits can still see what happened.
    - Print `[dcg] Allowing \`<pattern>\` → rebase-recovery mode
      (<label>)` to stderr so the agent *sees* the concession.
    - For `ActivePermit` reasons, call `consume_permit(cwd)` so
      it's truly single-shot.
    - `return` from `main` before the deny path executes.
  Guard rails: only fires when BOTH the matched pattern is
  recovery-appropriate AND a signal is active; outside that window
  the original deny path is byte-for-byte unchanged.

* `src/packs/core/git.rs` — extend the user-facing suggestions on
  the two affected rules (`checkout-discard`, `restore-worktree`)
  to teach users about recovery mode. Each block now ends with:

      Recovering from a failed `git pull --rebase`?
      Run `dcg rebase-recover` in this repo, then retry the command. This issues a
      short-lived, single-shot permit that unblocks this rule only. A rebase already
      in progress (`.git/rebase-merge/` or `.git/rebase-apply/` present) auto-allows
      the same rule without a permit.

  This keeps the error message self-documenting — users don't have
  to grep the README to discover the new subcommand.

No changes to other packs, other git rules, or the default-deny
contract outside the narrow recovery window. Closes #104.
